# Meme template catalog — generated by meme-seeder
# Format: INI-style, one section per template
# Compatible with you-get-a-meme's configparser loader
#
# Fields:
#   name           — display name
#   description    — semantic description for embedding search
#   caption_pattern — instructions for LLM caption generation
#   box_labels     — comma-separated labels, one per text box (in order)
#   tags           — comma-separated keywords
#   box_count      — number of text boxes in the template image


[0-days-without-lenny-simpsons]
name = 0 days without (Lenny, Simpsons)
description = Calling out something embarrassing, chaotic, or dysfunctional that keeps happening — probably just happened again. The image already shows '0' so the joke is in what fills the blank: the funnier and more specific, the better (e.g. 'having a meltdown', 'hitting a parked car', 'texting my ex').
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the incident): The thing that keeps happening — what fills in after 'DAYS WITHOUT'. Should be specific and funny. The '0' is already in the image, so don't reference the number.
box_labels = the incident
tags = recurring failure, zero days, keeps happening, here we go again, bad habit, relapse, again, announcement, self-aware, funny confession, incident counter, workplace chaos, chronic
box_count = 1
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/72epa9.png
tone = self-deprecating
notes = The "0 days without" text and the number 0 are baked into the template image — the user does NOT supply the number or the phrase, only what comes after it. Box 2 (culprit) is optional in many generators but commonly used to name a group being roasted. The humor relies entirely on the implied zero: the counter has already reset, meaning the failure is ongoing and inevitable. Common misuse: treating it like a generic announcement rather than specifically invoking the "reset to zero" failure joke.

[absolute-cinema]
name = Absolute Cinema
description = Used when something — a sports moment, a scene, a real-life event, or a hilariously mundane situation — is so dramatically over-the-top that it deserves to be called high art. The ironic use, declaring trivial things cinematic, now dominates over sincere use.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the subject): Describe the dramatic, epic, or absurdly mundane thing being reacted to — e.g. "my cat knocking a glass off the table in slow motion". Box 2 (the declaration): The punchline reaction — almost always "Absolute Cinema" or "ABSOLUTE CINEMA" for extra drama; can vary to "Kino" in film-nerd circles.
box_labels = the subject, the declaration
tags = admiration, ironic praise, hyperbole, sports, drama, film culture, reaction, over-the-top, cinematic, kino, scorsese, peak fiction
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/8d317n.png
tone = ironic
notes = The most widely shared variant has "Absolute Cinema" pre-baked as bottom text on the image — in that version box_count is effectively 1 (top text only). The blank/template version has both boxes user-fillable, which matches the current box_count of 2. The declaration box is almost always literally "Absolute Cinema"; the humor comes entirely from what is placed in the subject box. Sincere use (for genuinely great films or moments) exists but ironic use — applying the phrase to trivial or absurd things — now dominates. "Kino Is Served" is a related greentext spinoff from the same image.

[always-has-been]
name = Always Has Been
description = Used when you want to mock someone for being shocked by something that was obviously true the whole time, or to reveal that some uncomfortable, absurd, or disappointing thing has always been the underlying reality. The humor comes from the gun-wielding astronaut treating the revelation as old news while casually threatening to silence whoever just figured it out.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (shocking realization): The surprised astronaut's line — write as "Wait, it's all [X]?" where X is the thing that turns out to be pervasive or true (e.g. "Wait, it's all ads?"). Box 2 (always has been): The gun-wielding astronaut's deadpan reply — almost always just "Always has been."
box_labels = shocking realization, always has been
tags = ironic, revelation, uncomfortable truth, sarcasm, astronaut, gun, wait its all, cynical, dark humor, conspiracy, always has been, suppressed truth
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/46e43q.png
tone = ironic
notes = Box 1 is the unarmed (left/foreground) astronaut who has just noticed something alarming; Box 2 is the armed astronaut on the right who aims a gun in response — the implied threat is part of the joke, suggesting the truth is being suppressed. The phrase "Always has been" in Box 2 is user-supplied text, not baked into the image. A common misuse is putting the punchline in Box 1 — the reveal/realization always goes in Box 1, the deadpan confirmation in Box 2. The meme works best when X in Box 1 is something the audience half-suspected but finds darkly funny to confirm (e.g. "Wait, it's all just ads?", "Wait, it's all bots?").

[anakin-padme-4-panel]
name = Anakin Padme 4 Panel
description = Used when someone does something that sounds ambiguously ominous, and an optimistic onlooker assumes the best — only to realize no reassurance is coming. The humor comes from the gap between the naive hopeful reading and the dark reality silently confirmed by Anakin's smile.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (Anakin's statement): The action or decision being described — phrased neutrally but with an implied dark twist (e.g. "I rewrote the whole codebase", "We're cutting costs"). Box 2 (Padme hopeful): Padme's optimistic follow-up question assuming the best outcome, typically ending in "right?" (e.g. "In a way that makes things better, right?"). Box 3 (Padme worried): The same or similar question repeated by Padme with dread, after Anakin's silent non-answer confirms the worst (e.g. "...right?").
box_labels = anakin's statement, padme hopeful, padme worried
tags = dread, dark irony, silent confirmation, naive optimism shattered, ominous implication, no reassurance, star wars prequel, for the better right, uncomfortable truth, passive confirmation, unintended consequences, dark humor
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/5c7lwq.png
tone = ironic
notes = The image has 4 visual panels arranged in a 2x2 grid, but only 3 text boxes. Panel 3 (bottom-left) shows Anakin smiling silently with NO caption — this is the structural punchline and is baked into the visual. The silence IS the joke: Anakin neither confirms nor denies, which is itself the confirmation. Text boxes appear in order: top-left (panel 1, Anakin speaks), top-right (panel 2, Padme hopeful), bottom-right (panel 4, Padme worried). Common misuse: people sometimes add text to panel 3 (Anakin's smile), which breaks the format — the power comes from leaving it blank. The dialogue is entirely fabricated; none of it appears in the actual film.

[ancient-aliens]
name = Ancient Aliens
description = Used when you want to mock lazy, conspiratorial, or absurdly far-fetched reasoning by presenting a mystery or gap in knowledge and then confidently attributing it to the most ridiculous possible cause. The humor is the smug certainty of the nonsensical conclusion.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the unexplained thing): State the mysterious, confusing, or hard-to-explain phenomenon — e.g. "I don't know how she finished all her work so fast". Box 2 (the absurd explanation): Deliver the punchline explanation — usually just "ALIENS" or a variation like "it was aliens"; the more confidently stated, the funnier.
box_labels = the unexplained thing, the absurd explanation
tags = absurdist, conspiracy thinking, pseudoscience, unexplained mysteries, jumping to conclusions, alien, Giorgio Tsoukalos, Ancient Aliens, I'm not saying it was aliens, smug certainty, bad logic, non sequitur
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/26am.jpg
tone = absurdist
notes = The bottom text doesn't have to literally say "aliens" — the format works whenever the punchline is any wildly disproportionate or absurd explanation delivered with total confidence. A common variant has only one text box or no text box at all (just the image implying "aliens"), especially in comment threads where the image alone is the punchline. The humor is NOT about aliens specifically — it's about the rhetorical move of "I can't explain it, therefore [insane conclusion]." Over-literalizing this as only being about extraterrestrials is a common misuse.

[batman-slapping-robin]
name = Batman Slapping Robin
description = Used when someone says something so annoying, oblivious, or wrong that the only appropriate response is to cut them off mid-sentence. The meme captures the satisfaction of shutting down a bad take or dumb statement with zero patience.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (Robin's statement): What the person being shut down says — an innocent, oblivious, or irritating statement that is about to be interrupted; often trails off or feels incomplete since Batman cuts him off. Box 2 (Batman's slap): The forceful correction, dismissal, or counter-reaction that cuts off Box 1 mid-thought — often a correction, sarcastic retort, or statement that reveals why Box 1 was unacceptable.
box_labels = Robin's statement, Batman's shutdown
tags = frustration, correction, shutting someone down, disproportionate reaction, interruption, bad take, overreaction, cause and effect, dismissal, sarcastic, batman, callout
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/9ehk.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = Robin's speech bubble (Box 1) appears on the left and is always mid-sentence or incomplete — Batman slaps Robin before he finishes talking, so Box 1 often trails off or ends abruptly. Box 2 is Batman's dialogue as he delivers the slap. The classic original had Robin starting to ask about Christmas gifts and Batman interrupting with 'MY PARENTS ARE DEAAAD' — the template has since evolved far beyond that specific joke into any forceful correction or dismissal. The humor relies on Robin's statement being cut off, so Box 1 should read like the beginning of a thought, not a complete sentence.

[bell-curve]
name = Bell Curve
description = Used when you want to mock the overcomplicated, consensus-chasing "midwit" take on a topic by showing that the naive simple take and the galaxy-brained expert take land in exactly the same place — making the moderate look foolish for overthinking it.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (simple take): Write the blunt, naive, or low-effort conclusion — the obvious answer a non-expert would give, placed under the low-IQ left side of the curve. Box 2 (midwit cope): Write the overcomplicated or consensus-mainstream position that rejects Box 1 as too simple — this is the butt of the joke, placed under the peak of the curve. Box 3 (galaxy-brain take): Write the exact same conclusion as Box 1 (or nearly identical), implied to be arrived at through deep expertise — placed under the high-IQ right side of the curve.
box_labels = simple take, midwit cope, galaxy-brain take
tags = smugness, irony, contrarianism, dunning-kruger, midwit, iq bell curve, wojak, expertise vs ignorance, overthinking, nerd takedown, consensus mocking, galaxy brain
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/8tw3vb.png
tone = ironic
notes = The punchline depends on Boxes 1 and 3 saying the same (or nearly the same) thing — if they diverge the joke breaks. Box 2 must represent the "reasonable mainstream" position so that both extremes skewering it lands. The image contains three Wojak figures baked into the curve graphic (low-IQ brainlet on the left, distressed midwit in the center, serene hooded figure on the right) with no baked-in text labels, so all three boxes are free-form. Common misuse: people fill Box 3 with a genuinely different take rather than a mirror of Box 1, which flattens the joke into a simple "dumb vs smart" format and loses the core irony.

[bernie-i-am-once-again-asking-for-your-support]
name = Bernie I Am Once Again Asking For Your Support
description = Used when you or someone else is making the same tired, exasperated request for the nth time — especially when the ask is mundane, desperate, or absurdly relatable. The humor comes from framing any repeated plea in the deadpan sincerity of a presidential fundraising appeal.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (who is asking): The person or entity making the request — e.g. "My liver," "My bank account," "Me at 2am" — or left blank to let Bernie stand in for "me." Box 2 (what they keep asking for): The specific thing being requested, completing the implicit "I am once again asking for ___" — e.g. "one (1) molecule of serotonin," "you to do the dishes."
box_labels = who is asking, what they keep asking for
tags = frustration, repeated request, relatable, exasperation, self-deprecating, Bernie Sanders, political meme, desperation, once again asking, fundraising parody, deadpan, exhaustion
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/3oevdk.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = The original viral image has the full phrase "I am once again asking for your financial support" baked into it as a subtitle/chyron — making it technically a one-box or zero-box format where users just share it as-is. The editable two-box template (popularized on Imgflip and similar generators) is a derivative where users freely fill both boxes. Box 1 is often skipped or set to "me" — the punchline lives entirely in Box 2. The meme works best when Box 2 is something extremely mundane, emotionally needy, or petty, which creates comic contrast with the gravitas of a presidential campaign ad.

[bernie-sanders-once-again-asking]
name = Bernie Sanders Once Again Asking
description = Used when you've had to ask for the same thing repeatedly and are exasperated — or when framing a mundane, petty, or absurd request with the gravitas of a political fundraising plea. The humor comes from the gap between Sanders' weary formal sincerity and the trivial thing actually being asked for.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the asker): Who is asking — e.g. "me to my brain", "my dog", "your liver" — or left blank. Box 2 (what they keep asking for): The thing being desperately, repeatedly requested, completing the phrase "I am once again asking for your ___" — e.g. "one molecule of serotonin", "a nap".
box_labels = the asker, what they keep asking for
tags = frustration, exhaustion, repeated request, exasperation, relatable, bernie sanders, political, once again asking, fundraising parody, weary, deadpan, petty demands
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/3pdf2w.png
tone = self-deprecating
notes = The original video phrase is "I am once again asking for your financial support" — so Box 2 is the punchline that replaces "financial support". Some template variants bake in the full phrase "I AM ONCE AGAIN ASKING FOR YOUR" as image text, reducing user input to just the punchline noun; in those cases box_count is effectively 1. The most widely used blank template (Imgflip) has two free-floating boxes: top for the asker identity (often skipped or set to "me"), bottom for the thing requested. Box 1 is frequently left empty or set to a character label rather than a full sentence — the sentence structure lives in the meme's cultural memory, not the image itself.

[bike-fall]
name = Bike Fall
description = Used when someone causes their own problem through a self-defeating action and then blames something external for the predictable fallout. The humor comes from the obvious gap between the self-inflicted cause and the deflected blame.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the agent): Label who or what is riding along fine — the person, group, company, or ideology about to sabotage itself. Box 2 (the self-sabotage): Label the stick being inserted into the wheel — the specific dumb or counterproductive action they chose to take. Box 3 (the blame): Label what the crashed figure is blaming — the scapegoat held responsible instead of themselves.
box_labels = the agent, the self-sabotage, the scapegoat
tags = self-sabotage, irony, misplaced blame, hypocrisy, own worst enemy, frustration, schadenfreude, cause and effect, accountability, stick in wheel, baton roue, webcomic
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1b42wl.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = The key comedic beat is in Box 3, not Box 2 — the crash itself is expected, but the absurd deflection of blame is the punchline. Boxes appear top-to-bottom following panel order. A common misuse is labeling Box 3 with the consequence (the crash) rather than the blame/scapegoat; this loses the "zero accountability" humor logic the meme was built on. The original French webcomic (Baton Roue by Corentin Penloup, 2011) has no baked-in text, so all three boxes are free-form labels placed on each panel.

[boardroom-meeting-suggestion]
name = Boardroom Meeting Suggestion
description = Used when a sensible or consumer-friendly suggestion gets punished while bland, self-serving corporate alternatives are rewarded. Captures the frustration of institutions ignoring what people actually want.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (boss question): The authority figure's prompt — the topic or decision being put to the room (e.g. "How do we improve our app?"). Box 2 (safe answer 1): First employee's corporate-safe, acceptable non-answer (e.g. "Add more ads"). Box 3 (safe answer 2): Second employee's equally bland or self-serving suggestion (e.g. "Raise the subscription price"). Box 4 (punished suggestion): The third employee's honest, sensible, or consumer-approved idea — this person gets thrown out the window for saying it (e.g. "Make it free and open source").
box_labels = boss question, safe answer 1, safe answer 2, sensible suggestion
tags = frustration, irony, corporate satire, rejected idea, workplace humor, obvious solution ignored, meeting, boardroom, thrown out window, defenestration, consumer vs company, institutions
box_count = 4
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/m78d.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = The image is a 4-panel comic strip. Panel 4 shows BOTH the third employee making the sensible suggestion AND the consequence (being thrown out the window) — there is no separate fifth panel for the defenestration in the standard template, though extended versions exist. The humor inverts expectations: the worst ideas are praised, the best idea is violently rejected. Box 4 carries the entire punchline. Common misuse: putting the punished idea in box 2 or 3 instead of box 4, which breaks the escalating structure. The original (2012 Hejibits comic "Outlook Oust") was about Nintendo ignoring DVD playback on the Wii U.

[buff-doge-vs-cheems]
name = Buff Doge vs. Cheems
description = Used when contrasting two versions of the same thing — typically "then vs. now" or "old vs. new" — where one is glorified as strong and the other mocked as weak or pathetic. Works best when the contrast is exaggerated or self-deprecating.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (strong version label): The name or era of the impressive version (e.g. "Gamers in 2010", "My dad at 25"). Box 2 (strong version behavior): What that version does or is capable of — make it sound heroic or admirable. Box 3 (weak version label): The name or era of the sad version (e.g. "Gamers in 2020", "Me at 25"). Box 4 (weak version behavior): What the weak version does instead — make it pathetic, fragile, or clueless for comic effect.
box_labels = strong version label, strong version behavior, weak version label, weak version behavior
tags = self-deprecating, nostalgia, then vs now, comparison, decline, generational, swole doge, cheems, weakness, past vs present, modern fragility, virgin vs chad
box_count = 4
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/43a45p.png
tone = self-deprecating
notes = Boxes appear in reading order: top-left (strong label), bottom-left (strong behavior), top-right (weak label), bottom-right (weak behavior). The left side is always Buff/Swole Doge (strong), right side is always Cheems (weak) — reversing this breaks the joke. No text is baked into the image. A common misuse is putting the weak version on the left; the buff doge MUST be the thing being glorified. The meme is also sometimes called "Swole Doge vs. Cheems" — both names refer to the same template.

[change-my-mind]
name = Change My Mind
description = Used when asserting a bold, controversial, or absurd opinion as if it were an undeniable fact — the joke is the smug confidence of daring anyone to disagree. Works best when the claim is obviously wrong, hilariously trivial, or secretly true-but-taboo.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the claim): Write the bold, provocative, or absurd opinion being asserted — this is the text on the sign (e.g. "Cereal is just cold soup"). Box 2 (change my mind): This text is baked into the original image and requires no user input.
box_labels = the claim, change my mind (baked in)
tags = smugness, debate, hot take, unpopular opinion, challenge, absurdist claim, confident, controversial, dare, opinion, Steven Crowder, change my mind
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/24y43o.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = Box 2 ("CHANGE MY MIND") is baked into the original image as text on a physical sign — users only fill in Box 1 (the claim). Effective user-controlled box count is 1, not 2; box_count is set to 2 to reflect the actual number of labeled regions on the template. The humor works across a spectrum: sincere hot takes, obviously absurd claims (e.g. "birds are fruit"), and self-aware jokes all work. Misuse: using it for genuine debate-bait rather than humor kills the comedic tone.

[clown-applying-makeup]
name = Clown Applying Makeup
description = Used when someone justifies an increasingly absurd or self-defeating position through a chain of rationalizations that get dumber with each step, ending in full delusion. The humor is the self-aware escalation from reasonable to completely unhinged.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (reasonable premise): The initial statement or situation that sounds almost defensible — the seed of the delusion (e.g. "At least they're trying their best."). Box 2 (first rationalization): The first weak but follow-able excuse that starts the slide (e.g. "Besides, it's probably not that bad."). Box 3 (deeper delusion): A clearly strained justification that most people would call out — clown makeup visibly going on (e.g. "Actually this is good, actually."). Box 4 (full clown mode): The final, completely unhinged take — delivered with full commitment, now wearing the wig (e.g. "This is the best thing that's ever happened.").
box_labels = reasonable premise, first rationalization, deeper delusion, full clown mode
tags = self-delusion, escalation, rationalization, cope, bad takes, fandom defense, galaxy brain, irony, self-deprecating, making excuses, clown, sycophancy
box_count = 4
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/38el31.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = No text is baked into the image — all 4 boxes are fully user-defined. The panels run top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right (Z-order in a 2x2 grid), each showing a progressive stage of clown makeup application. Most commonly used in the first person (the poster calling themselves a clown) but also used to mock others' escalating bad arguments. Closely paired in usage with the Expanding Brain meme — both depict escalating logical absurdity, but Clown Makeup skews more toward emotional cope and fandom defense rather than pseudo-intellectual reasoning. Common misuse: treating box 4 as a standalone punchline caption rather than the logical endpoint of the escalation — all 4 boxes should form a connected chain.

[disaster-girl]
name = Disaster Girl
description = Used when someone is smugly responsible for — or secretly delighted by — a disaster unfolding around them. The humor is the contrast between serene, knowing satisfaction and utter catastrophe.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the disaster): Label the chaos, failure, or ongoing crisis in the background (e.g. "the codebase", "my diet", "the economy"). Box 2 (smug culprit): Name the innocent-looking cause or the person reveling in the destruction (e.g. "me after one bad PR").
box_labels = the disaster, smug culprit
tags = smugness, schadenfreude, chaos, secret satisfaction, dark humor, irony, mischief, complicity, disaster, self-incrimination, causing problems, girl meme
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/23ls.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = The original meme is a photoshop format — Disaster Girl's face is composited onto disaster images with no added text. The two-box caption version is a common adaptation where Box 1 labels what is burning or failing and Box 2 identifies the smug instigator (often "me"). Many examples use only a single caption or no text at all, relying on the image swap for the joke. The box ordering can feel reversed to new users: the disaster (context) comes first, the culprit (punchline) comes second.

[distracted-boyfriend]
name = Distracted Boyfriend
description = Used when someone abandons or ignores something sensible in favor of something new, shiny, or ill-advised. The humor is the self-aware confession of irrational temptation — the "I know I shouldn't but I am" feeling.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (tempting distraction): The new, appealing, or questionable thing being chased — label with a noun or short phrase. Box 2 (distracted boyfriend): The subject doing the neglecting — often "me" or a personified group or entity. Box 3 (ignored girlfriend): The sensible, existing obligation or option being left behind — label with a noun or short phrase.
box_labels = tempting distraction, distracted boyfriend, ignored girlfriend
tags = temptation, neglect, distraction, ironic, priorities, fomo, hypocrisy, desire, relatable, comparison, object labeling, commitment issues
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1ur9b0.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = Visual left-to-right order is: tempting distraction (woman in red, left) → distracted boyfriend (center, turning head) → ignored girlfriend (right, looking disapprovingly). A very common misuse is putting the boyfriend label on the distraction or reversing the girlfriend and distraction positions. The boyfriend box is typically labeled "me" in self-deprecating uses, or an entity/group in commentary uses. No text is baked into the image — all three boxes are free-form labels placed by the creator.

[drake-hotline-bling]
name = Drake Hotline Bling
description = Used when expressing a strong preference for one thing over another — rejecting the obvious or conventional choice in favor of something unexpected, niche, or self-aware. The joke lives in the contrast between the rejected and approved options.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (rejected option): The thing Drake is dismissing — the straightforward, expected, or "normal" choice. Box 2 (approved option): The thing Drake enthusiastically endorses — usually the punchline, the unexpected preference, or the petty/niche alternative.
box_labels = rejected option, approved option
tags = preference, comparison, rejection, approval, ironic, relatable, two-panel, drake, hotline bling, drakeposting, versus, petty
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/30b1gx.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = Panels are stacked vertically: rejected option on top, approved option on bottom. No text is baked into the image — both boxes are free-form. The comedic power comes from the approved option being surprising, petty, or unexpectedly specific; generic "bad vs good" labeling kills the joke. Also known as "Drakeposting." Drake's disapproving hand-gesture pose is panel 1 (top); his pointing/approving pose is panel 2 (bottom).

[epic-handshake]
name = Epic Handshake
description = Used when two seemingly different or opposing groups turn out to share one specific thing in common — highlighting hypocrisy, unexpected solidarity, or absurd common ground. The humor comes from the irony or surprise of the overlap.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (left arm): Label the first group, person, or faction. Box 2 (right arm): Label the second group, person, or faction. Box 3 (clasped hands): Write what both sides unexpectedly agree on or share — this is the punchline.
box_labels = left arm (first group), right arm (second group), clasped hands (shared thing)
tags = unexpected agreement, irony, hypocrisy, common ground, solidarity, object labeling, Predator handshake, Arnold Schwarzenegger, venn diagram, factions agreeing, two groups, surprising overlap
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/28j0te.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = The clasped hands box is the punchline — it should be the surprising or ironic shared thing, not a generic description. The two arm labels can be in either order (left/right doesn't carry meaning). Common misuse: making all three labels too obvious or earnest, which kills the humor — the best examples have an unexpected or hypocritical overlap. No text is baked into the original image; all three boxes are user-supplied. The image originates from a watercolor painting of the famous arm-wrestle handshake scene from the 1987 film Predator.

[expanding-brain]
name = Expanding Brain
description = Used when presenting a series of escalating takes where each step sounds smarter but the final one is absurdly over-engineered, silly, or contrarian — the joke being that the biggest brain always has the dumbest or most unhinged conclusion.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (basic take): The obvious, conventional answer — what any reasonable person would say. Box 2 (slightly smarter take): A marginally more sophisticated or pedantic version of the same idea. Box 3 (big brain take): An elaborate, unconventional, or contrarian take that sounds impressive but starts to feel suspicious. Box 4 (galaxy brain take): A completely unhinged, absurd, or stupidly simple conclusion framed as transcendent genius — this is the punchline and is almost always ironic.
box_labels = basic take, slightly smarter take, big brain take, galaxy brain take
tags = irony, escalation, absurdist, intellectual pretension, over-engineering, contrarian, galaxy brain, big brain, dumb logic, smugness, multi-panel, expanding brain
box_count = 4
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1jwhww.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = The meme is almost always ironic: the galaxy brain (final) panel is the punchline and is typically the dumbest, most ridiculous, or most absurdly overcomplicated option — framed as enlightenment. Panels are ordered top-to-bottom from small brain to largest/most glowing brain. No text is baked into the image; all four boxes are free-form. The format can be 2–8 panels, but 4 is the canonical standard. Common misuse: treating the final panel as a genuine "best" answer rather than a comedic inversion.

[grus-plan]
name = Gru's Plan
description = Used when a plan has one step that completely undermines or contradicts the whole thing — especially when the person executing it seems unaware at first, then has a dawning moment of horror. Captures the feeling of "wait, that defeats the entire purpose."
caption_pattern = Box 1 (step one): The first sensible, logical step — sets up that this is a reasonable endeavor. Box 2 (step two): A second reasonable step that builds on the first. Box 3 (step three — the plan): The step that completely contradicts or blows up the whole plan, written as if it's normal; Gru's expression is still confident. Box 4 (step three — the betrayal): Repeat the EXACT same text as Box 3 — this is intentional. Gru now stares back at the board in horror, having realized the flaw; identical text with a new reaction is the punchline.
box_labels = step one, step two, step three (plan), step three (betrayal)
tags = self-defeating, irony, flawed plan, unintended consequences, realization, self-sabotage, despicable me, gru, step by step, expectation vs reality, planning fail, facepalm
box_count = 4
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/26jxvz.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = The single most important rule: boxes 3 and 4 must contain IDENTICAL text. The joke lives entirely in the repetition — same words, but Gru's expression switches from confident to horrified. Beginners often write different text for box 4, which kills the joke. No text is baked into the template. Works for self-deprecating humor (mocking yourself) or third-party criticism (mocking a company, studio, political move, etc.). Panels 1–3 show Gru pointing at his plan confidently; panel 4 shows him looking back at the board in dismay.

[hide-the-pain-harold]
name = Hide the Pain Harold
description = Used when someone is visibly pretending everything is fine despite inner misery, exhaustion, or quiet desperation — the meme captures the universally relatable experience of forcing a smile through circumstances that absolutely do not deserve one.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the painful reality): Describe the uncomfortable, disappointing, or absurd situation being endured — often phrased as "when X" or a blunt statement of the miserable trigger (e.g. "when your boss asks you to stay late again"). Box 2 (forced smile response): Harold's internal reaction delivered as hollow acceptance or performative positivity — the "I'm fine, totally fine" energy that barely masks the suffering (e.g. "haha sure no problem :)" or "it's fine, everything is fine").
box_labels = the painful reality, forced smile response
tags = self-deprecating, forced positivity, quiet suffering, relatable, I'm fine, awkward, resigned, workplace, existential dread, harold, suppressed pain, fake smile
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/gk5el.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = No baked-in text — both boxes are fully user-defined. Box 1 sets up the painful situation (top), Box 2 delivers Harold's unconvincing acceptance (bottom). The humor lives entirely in the gap between the awful situation and the forced cheerfulness. Numerous template variants exist (Harold thumbs up, Harold with phone, Harold at doctor) but all follow the same 2-box logic. The meme is sometimes misused as a generic reaction image without the setup/punchline contrast, which loses the core joke. Harold's real name is András Arató, a Hungarian electrical engineer who has publicly embraced the meme.

[i-bet-hes-thinking-about-other-women]
name = I Bet He's Thinking About Other Women
description = Used when someone (or something) is assumed to be preoccupied with one thing, but is actually obsessing over something completely unrelated and absurd. The humor comes from the gap between the jealous/worried assumption and the hilariously mundane or nerdy reality.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (what she thinks he's thinking about): Write the jealous or worried assumption — typically "I bet he's thinking about other women" or a variation naming what the observer fears. Box 2 (what he's actually thinking about): Write the actual absurd, nerdy, or hyper-specific thought the man is really having — this is the punchline and should be completely unrelated to the assumed jealousy topic.
box_labels = I bet he's thinking about other women, what he's actually thinking about
tags = jealousy, subverted expectations, couples, absurdist humor, nerd culture, sports obsession, intrusive thoughts, irony, male stereotypes, relationship, thinking about
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1tl71a.jpg
tone = humorous
notes = Box 1 is overlaid on the woman's side of the image (she is turned toward the man with a worried look) and Box 2 is on the man's side (he is turned away, deep in thought). No text is baked into the image — both boxes are fully user-defined. The most common usage keeps Box 1 as the literal phrase "I bet he's thinking about other women" and makes Box 2 the actual joke. The meme is sometimes subverted with the roles reversed or used non-romantically (e.g., a country worrying about another country's military buildup while the other country is thinking about anime). The original meme started in Spanish ("seguro está pensando en otra") in 2016 before going viral in English in 2017.

[imagination-spongebob]
name = Imagination Spongebob
description = Used when you want to mock or celebrate the gap between a boring/disappointing reality and the grandiose or delusional spin you choose to put on it. The humor is in self-aware delusion — you know the truth is lame, but you're committed to the fantasy anyway.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (what it actually is): State the mundane, unflattering, or disappointing reality — the literal truth no one asked for. Box 2 (how I'm choosing to see it): Write the inflated, romanticized, or absurd reframing of that same reality — this goes on the rainbow and is the punchline.
box_labels = what it actually is, how I'm choosing to see it
tags = self-delusion, cope, reframing, optimism, sarcasm, absurdist, relatable, gap between reality and perception, spongebob, rainbow, imagination
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/3i7p.jpg
tone = absurdist
notes = The original viral format has "IMAGINATION" baked into the rainbow arc as part of the image, but this two-box generator variant replaces that with free text, making Box 2 the rainbow label itself. The punchline always lives in Box 2 — the setup in Box 1 is intentionally deflating so the rainbow reframe lands harder. Common misuse: putting the funny/grandiose text in Box 1 instead of Box 2 kills the comedic timing. The meme originates from SpongeBob S3E11 "The Idiot Box" (aired 2002).

[is-this-a-pigeon]
name = Is This A Pigeon
description = Use this when someone confidently misidentifies or miscategorizes something obvious — labeling a bad thing as good, a wrong solution as correct, or any situation where clueless confidence produces a hilariously wrong conclusion. The humor comes from the gap between what the thing actually is and what the oblivious person calls it.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the observer): Who or what is doing the misidentifying — a person, group, company, or concept that is confidently wrong (e.g. "me after 1 hour of coding", "marketing team", "my brain at 2am"). Box 2 (the thing): The actual thing being misidentified — what is really there, labeled honestly (e.g. "a bug I introduced yesterday", "burnout", "a coincidence"). Box 3 (wrong label): The incorrect name the observer gives it, phrased as a question — "Is this [wrong thing]?" (e.g. "Is this a feature?", "Is this productivity?", "Is this a sign I should quit?").
box_labels = the observer, the thing, wrong label
tags = misidentification, oblivious, confident wrongness, irony, relatable, self-deprecating, confusion, labeling, miscategorization, anime, clueless, calling something the wrong thing
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1o00in.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = The original anime screencap has the subtitle "Is this a pigeon?" baked in, but the widely-used meme template is blank — all three labels are user-supplied. Visually the butterfly appears at the top and the person at the bottom-left, but box order by convention follows the joke's narrative: observer first, misidentified thing second, wrong name third. Box 3 is almost always phrased as a question ("Is this a ___?") to mirror the source material. Common misuse: people sometimes put the wrong label on box 2 and the actual thing on box 3, reversing the punchline logic.

[left-exit-12-off-ramp]
name = Left Exit 12 Off Ramp
description = Use this when you want to joke about dramatically abandoning the sensible or expected option in favor of something you clearly prefer, even if it's irrational, chaotic, or self-sabotaging. It captures the feeling of making an impulsive swerve toward temptation while ignoring the responsible path.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (who is choosing): Label on the car — typically "me" or whoever/whatever is making the dramatic swerve. Box 2 (ignored option): Text on the straight-ahead highway sign — the responsible, expected, or boring choice being abandoned. Box 3 (chosen option): Text on the exit-ramp sign — the tempting, absurd, or chaotic option being swerved toward.
box_labels = who is choosing, ignored option, chosen option
tags = impulsive decision, temptation, self-sabotage, bad choices, swerving, preference, irrationality, highway, car meme, relatable, avoidance, choosing chaos
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/22bdq6.jpg
tone = humorous
notes = The car label (box 1) appears lower in the image than the sign labels, but logically it represents the subject/agent making the choice, so it is conventionally listed first in caption templates. Box 2 (ignored option) is on the left/straight-ahead sign, and box 3 (chosen option) is on the right/exit-ramp sign — the car visibly swerves hard toward box 3. A common misuse is swapping boxes 2 and 3, making the meme read backwards (car appearing to choose the straight road). No text is baked into the original image; all three boxes are fully user-defined.

[marked-safe-from]
name = Marked Safe From
description = Used when you want to mock the seriousness of Facebook's disaster Safety Check feature by declaring yourself "safe" from something trivially annoying or absurd — a bad meeting, a spoiler, a Monday, your ex. The humor comes from treating mundane nuisances like genuine emergencies.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the thing avoided): The specific annoyance, event, trend, or situation you're hilariously claiming to have survived — e.g. "Avengers: Endgame spoilers", "a 2-hour Zoom meeting", "the group chat", "Monday". Keep it punchy; the joke lands on what fills this blank.
box_labels = the thing avoided
tags = relief, survival humor, Facebook parody, Safety Check, everyday annoyances, irony, first-world problems, disaster parody, mundane emergencies, social media satire, marked safe
box_count = 1
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/2odckz.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = Heavy baked-in text: the blue flag icon, the "Marked Safe From" header, and "Today" footer are all part of the image — users only fill in the one blank naming what was avoided. The "who was spared" is always implicitly the poster (inherited from Facebook's Safety Check UI), so it is NOT a separate text box. Some generator variants add a second optional field for a name/date, but the canonical single-field version is standard. The humor logic is entirely about the gap between the gravity of the format (used for earthquakes, hurricanes) and the triviality of what fills the blank.

[megamind-peeking]
name = Megamind peeking
description = Used to smugly mock or call out someone for lacking something — social life, skill, basic habits, a relationship — often in response to someone displaying classic "no life" or try-hard behavior. The humor comes from Megamind's nervous, judgmental peek being a stand-in for the audience catching someone red-handed being pathetic.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the subject being called out): Name or describe the person or behavior being mocked — e.g. "someone who moderates 12 Discord servers" or just a label like "you". Box 2 (the punchline question): The thing they're lacking or the condescending question, formatted as "No [thing]?" — e.g. "No bitches?" or "No friends?" or "No sleep schedule?"
box_labels = subject being called out, condescending "No [X]?" question
tags = smug, mockery, condescending, no bitches, calling out, social commentary, loser behavior, passive aggressive, Megamind, peeking, no life, ironic judgment
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/64sz4u.png
tone = sarcastic
notes = The original viral form was a single-caption image — just "No Bitches?" overlaid on Megamind peeking, with no explicit two-box split. The two-box template adds a setup (Box 1) to name the target before landing the punchline question (Box 2). Some users run it as a single-box meme with just "No [X]?" and no setup label at all. Box ordering: setup on top, punchline on bottom. Do not confuse with the unrelated "No Peeking" meme template.

[mocking-spongebob]
name = Mocking Spongebob
description = Use this meme when you want to mock, dismiss, or ridicule something someone said by repeating it back in a condescending, parroting tone. It captures the feeling of sarcastically imitating someone to signal their statement is stupid or deserves contempt.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (original statement): The real, straight-faced statement, opinion, or complaint being mocked — written normally. Box 2 (mocking echo): The same statement repeated back in alternating uppercase and lowercase letters (e.g. "i NeEd HeLtHcArE") to convey ridicule and dismissal.
box_labels = original statement, mocking echo
tags = sarcasm, mockery, ridicule, dismissal, parroting, condescending, alternating caps, spongebob, spongemock, reaction, call and response, irony
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1otk96.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = The defining feature of this meme is the alternating-capitalization text in box 2 (e.g. "lIkE tHiS") — the visual distortion IS the joke; without it the meme loses its meaning. Box 1 is always the sincere version of the statement; box 2 is always the mocking repeat. The image itself has no baked-in text. Common misuse: some people put an unrelated punchline in box 2 instead of echoing box 1, which breaks the parroting format the meme is built on.

[monkey-puppet]
name = Monkey Puppet
description = Used when you're caught seeing something you shouldn't have noticed, or when an uncomfortable truth surfaces that directly applies to you and the natural instinct is to look away and pretend it didn't happen. The humor comes from the relatable guilt of the side-eye — everyone knows you saw it.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the awkward moment): The uncomfortable thing that just happened or was said — the situation, message, or fact that puts you on the spot (e.g. "when the group chat asks who ate the last slice and you did"). Box 2 (trying to ignore it): What you (the puppet) are doing in response — pretending not to notice, playing dumb, or avoiding eye contact (e.g. "me slowly looking away").
box_labels = the awkward moment, trying to ignore it
tags = awkward, embarrassment, avoidance, side-eye, caught, guilty, relatable, reaction, social anxiety, pretending not to notice, monkey puppet, pedro puppet
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/2gnnjh.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = Box 1 sets up the situation (the thing that implicates you), Box 2 is the side-eye reaction (you pretending you didn't see it). Both boxes are often replaced by a single caption below the full two-panel image describing the whole scenario — this is also valid. The meme originated in Spanish-speaking internet culture as "No ahora, por favor" before spreading in English. The side-eye in panel 2 was Photoshopped — the original show footage only has the straight-ahead look.

[mother-ignoring-kid-drowning-in-a-pool]
name = Mother Ignoring Kid Drowning In A Pool
description = Used when someone (a person, company, algorithm, or system) visibly ignores an important thing in favor of something less important — and the format lets you escalate the joke by implying there's also a completely forgotten thing rotting at the bottom. The humor comes from misplaced priorities taken to absurd extremes.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the ignorer): The person, company, system, or entity doing the ignoring — e.g. "YouTube's algorithm", "my brain", "Nintendo". Box 2 (what they care about): The thing getting all the attention — the favored, lesser, or silly priority — e.g. "low-effort 10-minute videos", "anxiety", "the Switch". Box 3 (what they're ignoring): The thing being neglected right now, visibly suffering — e.g. "well-edited short videos", "sleep", "the 3DS". Box 4 (meanwhile / stakes): The thing that was neglected so long it's already dead — the skeleton at the bottom of the pool — e.g. "actually original content", "my mental health", "the Wii U".
box_labels = the ignorer, what they care about, what they're ignoring, meanwhile / stakes
tags = misplaced priorities, neglect, irony, sarcasm, object labeling, escalation, forgotten, priorities meme, pool meme, skeleton, corporate criticism, hypocrisy
box_count = 4
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/46hhvr.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = The skeleton panel (box 4) was added in 2020 and is what makes this a 4-box template rather than 3 — without it, it's just mother + two kids. The visual order matters: the drowning kid (box 3, being neglected NOW) appears in the pool background, the favored kid (box 2) is being held up front, the mother (box 1) is center, and the skeleton (box 4) is a separate panel below the entire pool scene. Many users treat box 4 as optional escalation — "the thing that already died from neglect" — which is where the best jokes land. The original image is a genuine 1980s family photo, not staged.

[one-does-not-simply]
name = One Does Not Simply
description = Used when something looks easy but has hidden complexity, absurd obstacles, or is just hilariously harder than it should be. The joke lands by framing an everyday frustration or impossible task in the gravely serious tone of a fantasy epic.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the setup): Almost always literally "ONE DOES NOT SIMPLY" — keep this fixed unless doing a creative variant. Box 2 (the difficult thing): Complete the sentence with the action that is deceptively difficult, e.g. "WALK INTO MORDOR", "JUST RESTART THE COMPUTER", or "EAT ONE CHIP" — Box 1 and Box 2 should read as one continuous statement.
box_labels = one does not simply, the difficult thing
tags = frustration, irony, difficulty, impossible task, Lord of the Rings, Boromir, fantasy, snowclone, complexity, understatement, relatable struggle, hidden obstacle
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1bij.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = Box 1 is technically user-supplied text, but by overwhelming convention it is always "ONE DOES NOT SIMPLY" — changing it breaks the meme. The real creative work happens entirely in Box 2. No text is baked into the image. Common misuse: treating Box 2 as a punchline that doesn't grammatically complete the sentence from Box 1 — it should always read as one continuous statement: "[Box 1] [Box 2]". The original film quote ends "walk into Mordor" but the meme template lets users replace that entire phrase.

[oprah-you-get-a]
name = Oprah You Get A
description = Used when you want to comically declare that absolutely everyone is getting something — wanted or not. The humor comes from applying Oprah's over-the-top generosity energy to mundane, absurd, or even terrible things being distributed universally.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the giveaway): What the thing is that everyone is receiving — written in all-caps Oprah-style hype, e.g. "YOU GET A FLU!" or "YOU GET A BAN!". Box 2 (the crowd): Optionally label the recipients or extend the joke with a second beat, e.g. "EVERYBODY GETS [X]!" or "AND YOU GET [X]! AND YOU GET [X]!" — leave blank if Box 1 carries the full joke.
box_labels = the giveaway, everybody gets
tags = excitement, generosity, universal distribution, hyperbole, sarcasm, Oprah, you get a car, everyone gets, absurdist humor, chaotic energy, pop culture reference, mass giveaway
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/gtj5t.jpg
tone = humorous
notes = The image shows Oprah with arms raised triumphantly on stage — no text is baked into the image, so both boxes are fully user-defined. The meme works equally well for genuinely good things (upvotes, Wi-Fi bars) and darkly comic bad things (the flu, a ban, debt). The joke lands harder when the distributed thing is mundane or undesirable — the contrast with Oprah's unbridled enthusiasm is the punchline. Some variants repeat the announcement line multiple times before a closing "everybody gets" line, but the standard two-box template condenses this into top/bottom.

[pawn-stars-best-i-can-do]
name = Pawn Stars Best I Can Do
description = Used when someone — a person, institution, or life itself — offers something pathetically inadequate compared to what was asked for. Perfect for negotiation failures, disappointing compromises, and situations where the offer is technically an answer but misses the point entirely.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (what you want): State the reasonable, desirable thing being asked for — framed as a price, request, expectation, or need (e.g. "I need $500 to cover rent"). Box 2 (best I can do): Write the lowball counter-offer — something absurdly insufficient, conventionally introduced with "Best I can do is..." (e.g. "Best I can do is $3 and a granola bar").
box_labels = what you want, best I can do
tags = frustration, disappointment, negotiation, lowball offer, expectation vs reality, Pawn Stars, Rick Harrison, bad deal, haggling, underpaid, reality check, compromise fail
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/19vcz0.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = No baked-in text — both boxes are fully user-defined. The phrase "Best I can do is" is conventionally written into Box 2 by the user, not pre-printed on the image. Box 1 frames what you want or deserve; Box 2 is the punchline. The humor works best when the gap between the two is maximum — the offer should feel insultingly small or irrelevant relative to the ask. Originally used for literal low-price negotiations but now commonly applied to salary, health, relationships, government services, and absurdist scenarios.

[roll-safe-think-about-it]
name = Roll Safe Think About It
description = Used when someone presents a technically-true but completely absurd "solution" to a problem — the kind of flawed logic that avoids a bad outcome by bypassing the situation entirely rather than actually fixing it. The humor comes from the character's smug expression paired with hilariously bad reasoning.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the problem): State the negative outcome or real-world problem you want to avoid — e.g. "being broke", "getting fired", "failing an exam". This is sometimes left blank when the problem is embedded directly in Box 2's phrasing. Box 2 (the big-brain solution): Deliver the absurd, illogical workaround that technically sidesteps Box 1 — most commonly phrased as "You can't [problem] if you [ridiculous avoidance]" (e.g. "can't be broke if you never check your bank account").
box_labels = the problem, the big-brain solution
tags = smugness, flawed logic, galaxy brain, avoidance, ironic advice, can't lose if, deadpan, relatable failure, self-congratulatory, problem solving, think about it, roll safe
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1h7in3.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = The two text boxes appear above and below the image respectively (standard image macro layout — no text is baked into the image). The key humor mechanic is that the "solution" in Box 2 doesn't actually solve the problem — it sidesteps it entirely. The most viral examples follow a strict "You can't [bad thing] if [you remove the precondition for the bad thing]" formula, so Box 1 is often implicit within Box 2's phrasing. Some creators compress both parts into a single bottom caption and leave the top blank, but the intended two-box format has Box 1 setting up the premise and Box 2 delivering the punchline.

[running-away-balloon]
name = Running Away Balloon
description = Used when something you want feels tantalizingly close but some force — internal or external — keeps dragging you away from it. The humor comes from dramatizing the gap between aspiration and the embarrassingly mundane thing sabotaging it.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the balloon): The desirable goal, thing, or state being chased — e.g. "my gym routine", "sleep", "productivity". Box 2 (the runner): Who or what is doing the chasing — usually "me" or a relatable subject. Box 3 (what pulls them back): The force dragging the runner away from the balloon — the villain of the piece, e.g. "my bed at 6am", "43 homework assignments", "social anxiety".
box_labels = the balloon (desired thing), the runner (me), what pulls them back
tags = relatable, self-deprecating, aspiration vs reality, internal conflict, obstacle, thwarted goals, frustration, procrastination, desire, held back, FOMO, running away
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/261o3j.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = The image has no baked-in text — all three labels are user-added. Visually, the balloon floats upper-left, the grey child runs toward it in the middle, and the pink blob grabs the child from the right/below. Label order follows that spatial layout: goal first, then the pursuer, then the blocker. A common misuse is labeling only the balloon and the pink blob (treating it as a two-box format), which loses the explicit "me" label that makes the self-deprecating joke land. The original comic labeled the balloon "opportunities" and the pink figure "shyness" — but internet usage quickly broadened to any goal/obstacle pairing, often absurdist or hyperbolic for comedic effect.

[sad-pablo-escobar]
name = Sad Pablo Escobar
description = Used when you've been stood up, ghosted, or left waiting for something that never arrived — capturing the pathetic, resigned boredom of being the one left hanging while everyone else moved on. Works best when the thing being waited on is mundane or embarrassing to admit you cared about.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (who/what didn't show up): Name the specific thing or person you were waiting for — e.g. "my motivation", "her text back", "the wifi". Box 2 (still waiting): Caption with elapsed time or a second beat of the same joke — e.g. "still waiting", "day 3". Box 3 (the empty situation): The punchline — what you're stuck with instead, e.g. "nothing", "just vibes", "this empty pool". Many users leave boxes 2 and 3 blank and let the image carry the pathos.
box_labels = who/what didn't show up, still waiting, the empty situation
tags = waiting, ghosted, stood up, boredom, loneliness, disappointment, anticipation, ignored, Pablo Escobar, Narcos, relatable, self-deprecating
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1c1uej.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = The three panels are visually distinct settings (outdoor field, kitchen table, empty pool), arranged left-to-right or top-to-bottom depending on the crop — no baked-in text on the image itself, so all 3 boxes are free-form. In practice, many users treat this as effectively a 1-box meme: they write a single caption on panel 1 (or as a header above all three panels) and let the visual progression speak for itself. The funniest executions contrast a very trivial thing being waited on with Escobar's theatrically grim expression. Occasionally used as a 2-panel crop (just the field shot alone). Do not confuse with the separate "Narcos Pablo Escobar" reaction memes — this specific template is always the 3-panel waiting sequence.

[soldier-protecting-sleeping-child]
name = Soldier protecting sleeping child
description = Used when something is heroically — or pathetically — absorbing punishment to shield something else you care about. The humor comes from the absurd mismatch between the epic military framing and mundane or embarrassing subject matter. Also works sincerely for unsung-protector appreciation posts.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (incoming threats): The dangers or bad things being absorbed — e.g. "crippling loneliness", "deadlines", "spoilers". Box 2 (the protector): The thing heroically standing between threat and precious thing — e.g. "caffeine", "denial", "my headphones". Box 3 (what's being protected): The precious, fragile, or embarrassing thing being kept safe — e.g. "my last brain cell", "my will to live", "waifus".
box_labels = incoming threats, the protector, what's being protected
tags = protection, sacrifice, heroic, absurdist, object labeling, coping, irony, unsung hero, self-deprecating humor, relatable struggle, guardian, silent protector
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/2tzo2k.jpg
tone = absurdist
notes = Box ordering matters: the threats/attacks (Box 1) appear visually first as the incoming force, the soldier (Box 2) is the central figure absorbing them, and the child (Box 3) is the sleeping figure in the background being shielded. A common misuse is labeling Box 1 as the protector — the soldier is always Box 2. There is also a "Soldier failing to protect sleeping child" variant (separate template) where the soldier is dead/fallen, used for "I tried but failed to protect X from Y." No text is baked into the image.

[squidward-window]
name = Squidward window
description = Used when you want to express FOMO, jealousy, or the feeling of being stuck on the outside looking in while others get to enjoy something you can't have or aren't part of. Captures the specific mix of longing and mild resentment of a self-aware outsider.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the watcher): Label Squidward — the excluded, envious, or left-out party (e.g. "me", "Android users", "broke people"). Box 2 (what they're watching): Label SpongeBob and Patrick together as the thing or group the watcher envies or is locked out of (e.g. "my friends on vacation", "people with iPhones", "fun").
box_labels = the watcher, what they're watching
tags = jealousy, FOMO, envy, exclusion, longing, missing out, outsider, left out, SpongeBob, Squidward, relatable frustration, have-nots
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/145qvv.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = SpongeBob and Patrick are almost always treated as a single unit (one label), making this effectively a 2-box meme despite three visible characters. Some creators do split SpongeBob and Patrick into separate labels for 3-way comparisons (e.g. brand rivalries), but that is the less common usage. No text is baked into the image. The emotional core is jealous longing rather than pure anger — Squidward wants to be out there, not just opposed to it.

[theyre-the-same-picture]
name = They're The Same Picture
description = Used when two things are treated as distinct but are functionally identical — the meme mocks the false distinction with deadpan corporate logic. Works as a burn (these are equally bad) or a compliment (these are equally great).
caption_pattern = Box 1 (presenter label): Optional — name the person, brand, or system making the absurd comparison (e.g. "My brain at 2am", "Every tech CEO") or leave blank. Box 2 (thing A): Label for the first item being compared — a word or short phrase. Box 3 (thing B): Label for the supposedly different but actually identical counterpart. The punchline "They're the same picture" is baked into the image and should not be typed as a text box.
box_labels = presenter (optional), thing A, thing B
tags = irony, comparison, false distinction, sarcasm, equivalence, The Office, Pam Halpert, calling out, same thing, roast, two things identical, corporate speak
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/2za3u1.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = The punchline — "They're the same picture" — is baked into the bottom panel and should never be typed as a text box. The presenter label (Box 1) is frequently left blank; when used, it names the entity making the absurd comparison (often self-referential). The two comparison items are visually placed side by side in the top panel. Misuse: some creators add a fourth box restating the punchline, which is redundant since it's already in the image. Origin: The Office S07E24 "Search Committee" (2011), went exploitable in 2018.

[this-is-fine]
name = This Is Fine
description = Used when someone is visibly, catastrophically failing or overwhelmed but is choosing — consciously or not — to pretend everything is normal. The humor comes from the absurd gap between how bad things actually are and the serene, resigned non-reaction.
caption_pattern = Most often used as a pure reaction image with no text — the phrase "This is fine" is baked into the original comic art. When labels are added: Box 1 (the coper): label whoever is maintaining false calm — e.g. "me", "my brain", "the dev team". Box 2 (the disaster): label the catastrophic situation being cheerfully ignored — e.g. "production is down", "the economy", "my sleep schedule".
box_labels = the coper, the disaster
tags = denial, cope, resignation, burnout, disaster, self-deprecating, calm in chaos, irony, stress, overwhelmed, reaction image, everything is fine
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/wxica.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = The phrase "This is fine" is baked into the original comic artwork — this is not a blank template with text boxes. The meme is most commonly deployed as a zero-text reaction image. When custom labels are added, they identify the dog (the coper) and the fire/room (the disaster), but this is a secondary usage. The box_count of 2 reflects the labeling variant, not a standard fill-in format. Misuse: people sometimes add top/bottom caption text describing the scenario above or below the image rather than labeling the dog and fire directly.

[this-is-where-id-put-my-trophy-if-i-had-one]
name = This Is Where I'd Put My Trophy If I Had One
description = Used when you want to highlight the painful absence of something you wish you had — an achievement, recognition, relationship, or resource — by pointing to the empty space where it should be. The humor comes from the theatrical self-pity of gesturing at nothing.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (place for it): Name the specific thing you lack and would proudly display, e.g. "my work-life balance" or "my social life" or "my Valentine's Day plans." Box 2 (if I had one): The punchline — almost always "IF I HAD ONE", left verbatim or lightly customized to match Box 1's subject.
box_labels = place for it, if I had one
tags = self-deprecating, frustration, absence, fairly oddparents, mr turner, lacking achievement, recognition, empty shelf, wish i had, resignation, relatable, if i had one
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1wz1x.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = From The Fairly OddParents episode "Father Time" (2001) — Mr. Turner gestures at an empty shelf where a childhood marathon trophy would go. The phrase "IF I HAD ONE" is the iconic verbal punchline from the source clip; on most meme generators it becomes Box 2 and is often left unchanged or only lightly modified. Box 1 does the real creative work — it names whatever the user feels they're painfully missing. Common misuse is overexplaining Box 1 when a short, punchy noun phrase lands better. Box 2 label was corrected from 'the thing i don't have' to 'if I had one' to match the actual verbal punchline.

[trade-offer]
name = Trade Offer
description = Used when you want to call out a one-sided deal where someone (a company, person, or abstract force) gets everything and you get nothing — or worse. The humor comes from framing an obviously unfair arrangement as if it were a legitimate business proposal.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (offeror label): Who or what is making the offer — e.g. "Netflix", "My Anxiety", "The Gym". Box 2 (I receive): What the offeror gains — always the good end of the deal, stated plainly. Box 3 (you receive): What you get — usually nothing, something painful, or something absurd; the bigger the imbalance vs. Box 2, the funnier.
box_labels = offeror label, I receive, you receive
tags = unfair deal, one-sided exchange, corporate greed, bad trade, negotiation, smugness, frustration, calling out, lopsided, roblox trade, I receive you receive, trade proposal
box_count = 3
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/54hjww.jpg
tone = sarcastic
notes = The "TRADE OFFER" warning banner with warning symbols is baked into the image — users do not fill it in. The offeror label (Box 1) is typically placed above or below the man's image and names who's proposing the deal. The joke structure requires Box 2 ("I receive") to be the clearly better deal; Box 3 ("you receive") is the punchline — it should be hilariously bad or pathetically small compared to Box 2. Common misuse: swapping which side is advantaged defeats the joke. The format originated from TikToker @bradeazy's March 2021 video and mimics Roblox's in-game trade UI.

[tuxedo-winnie-the-pooh]
name = Tuxedo Winnie The Pooh
description = Used when you want to present two versions of the same thing — one phrased plainly and one dressed up as sophisticated or refined — to mock pretentiousness or sincerely endorse the "classier" option. The joke is that tuxedo Pooh's smug approval elevates mundane or even trashy choices into high culture.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (lowbrow version): The plain, basic, or "common" phrasing of a thing — how a normal person would describe it, e.g. "saying no", "getting fired", "eating cereal for dinner". Box 2 (elevated version): The same thing reworded with unnecessary sophistication, or a genuinely superior alternative endorsed with smug approval, e.g. "a formal declination", "being promoted to customer", "an artisanal grain-based evening repast".
box_labels = lowbrow version, elevated version
tags = smugness, superiority, pretentiousness, irony, class comparison, two-panel, man of culture, refined, fancy pooh, a fellow man of culture, comparison meme, self-satisfaction
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/2ybua0.png
tone = sarcastic
notes = Box 1 (top) is regular Pooh in his red shirt — the "basic" option. Box 2 (bottom) is tuxedo Pooh reclining with a smug expression — the "refined" endorsement. The meme works both sincerely (bottom truly is better) and ironically (bottom is equally dumb but said fancier). The phrase "A Fellow Man of Culture" often appears as a caption or title. A 3-panel variant exists where a third even-more-elaborate Pooh represents a peak absurdist option — but the standard template is 2 panels. No text is baked into the image.

[two-buttons]
name = Two Buttons
description = Used when someone faces two contradictory options they both want, both hate, or can't logically hold at the same time — capturing the absurd agony of an impossible or self-defeating choice. The humor comes from externalizing the ridiculous internal conflict, often exposing hypocrisy or relatable indecision.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (left button): One of the two conflicting options, desires, beliefs, or impulses — usually the one that contradicts the other. Box 2 (right button): The opposing option, often equally appealing, equally bad, or logically incompatible with Box 1. The sweating person in the image is a fixed visual with no text box; he represents whoever is caught in the dilemma.
box_labels = left button, right button
tags = indecision, impossible choice, dilemma, hypocrisy, self-contradiction, both options, daily struggle, sweating man, relatable, internal conflict, frustration, choice paralysis
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1g8my4.jpg
tone = humorous
notes = The original template (Jake Clark, 2014) has only 2 text boxes — one label per button. The sweating/distressed person below the buttons is a fixed image element with no associated text box; he visually represents whoever is caught in the dilemma but is not captioned. A common misuse is treating him as a third text box (e.g. labeling him 'me' or a group name), which is not part of the standard template but appears in some generator tools as an optional addition. A popular spin-off 'Both Buttons' variant shows the person pressing both buttons simultaneously, used to express refusing to choose. The meme is also known as 'Daily Struggle' on Know Your Meme.

[uno-draw-25-cards]
name = UNO Draw 25 Cards
description = Used when someone stubbornly refuses to do something simple or mildly uncomfortable and instead chooses a far worse alternative — capturing the humor of irrational avoidance. The punchline is that drawing 25 cards is objectively worse than the avoided action, making the refusal absurd and relatable.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (avoided option): Write the thing being avoided as a UNO card prompt, e.g. "You can [do the thing] or Draw 25" — this goes on the UNO wild card in the left panel. Box 2 (drawing 25 cards): Label who or what is choosing to draw instead of doing the thing, e.g. "Me" or the subject of the joke — this labels the right panel showing the man holding a huge hand of cards.
box_labels = avoided option, drawing 25 cards
tags = avoidance, refusal, irrational choice, procrastination, self-sabotage, relatable, frustration, stubbornness, UNO, card game, dilemma, obvious choice
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/3lmzyx.jpg
tone = humorous
notes = The UNO card in the left panel has "Draw 25" baked into the card's design as part of the image — users only fill in the option being avoided (the top part of the card prompt). The right panel image of the man with a huge hand of cards requires no text to land the joke; Box 2 is often just "Me" or the subject's name. The humor works because drawing 25 cards is objectively the worse outcome, making the avoidance absurd. A common misuse is putting the punchline in Box 1 — the avoided thing should always be the mildly unpleasant or simple action, not the dramatic one. Originated on Facebook in late 2019, went viral on Twitter in January 2020 with the "text your ex or Draw 25" version.

[waiting-skeleton]
name = Waiting Skeleton
description = Used when something takes so absurdly long that you'd have turned to bones before it arrives. The humor comes from the skeleton itself being the punchline — the image implies you've been waiting so long you've died and decomposed.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (subject + thing being waited for): A single caption naming who is waiting and what they're waiting for, e.g. "Me waiting for my professor to post the syllabus" or "Android users waiting for the group chat to include them." The skeleton's appearance supplies the "it's been forever" punchline implicitly — no second box needed.
box_labels = subject + thing being waited for
tags = waiting, patience, impatience, frustration, relatable, procrastination, overdue, anticipation, skeleton, forever, self-deprecating, delay
box_count = 1
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/2fm6x.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = The meme template has only ONE text box in practice, despite some generators defaulting to two. The skeleton itself IS the "how long" answer — the bony decomposed figure implies an eternity of waiting, so no explicit duration text is needed or conventionally used. Common caption structure: "[Me / group] waiting for [thing]" — the absurdity of the wait is shown, not told.

[woman-yelling-at-cat]
name = Woman Yelling At Cat
description = Used when you want to contrast someone's intense, over-the-top accusation or demand with a completely unbothered, innocent, or clueless response. The humor comes from the absurd mismatch between the emotional energy of the accuser and the calm indifference of the accused.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (yelling woman): The accusation, complaint, or emotional outburst — what someone is furiously blaming or demanding; this is the dramatic, irrational, or disproportionate side. Box 2 (unbothered cat): The response of the thing being accused — calm, confused, innocent, or completely indifferent; often a simple denial, a shrug, or an absurdly reasonable counter.
box_labels = yelling woman, unbothered cat
tags = frustration, accusation, indifference, overreaction, argument, two sides, conflict, smudge cat, taylor armstrong, real housewives, versus, debate
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/345v97.jpg
tone = humorous
notes = The yelling woman panel is on the LEFT and the cat (Smudge) is on the RIGHT — this ordering is fixed and well-known, so swapping them would confuse viewers. The woman represents the accuser/the outraged party; the cat represents whoever or whatever is being blamed and is totally unbothered. A common misuse is putting the calm/reasonable party on the left — the meme only lands when the left panel is the irrational or emotionally heightened side. No baked-in text; both boxes are fully user-defined.

[x-x-everywhere]
name = X, X Everywhere
description = Used when something suddenly seems annoyingly or absurdly ubiquitous — once you notice it, you can't stop seeing it. The humor comes from Buzz's wide-eyed realization that yes, this thing really is everywhere.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the thing): The noun or phrase that is everywhere, written twice separated by a comma — e.g. "Ads, ads" or "Meetings, meetings". Box 2 (it's everywhere): The word "everywhere" or a short completion like "everywhere you look" — this finishes the sentence started in Box 1.
box_labels = the thing, it's everywhere
tags = frustration, irony, ubiquity, saturation, overabundance, noticing a trend, Toy Story, Buzz Lightyear, suddenly everywhere, annoyance, observation humor, repetition
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/1ihzfe.jpg
tone = ironic
notes = The classic format splits the phrase across two boxes: Box 1 holds the repeated noun (e.g. "Bugs, bugs"), Box 2 holds "everywhere". Neither box has text baked into the base image — both are fully user-supplied. The meme originates from a 420chan wordfilter in 2007 and became mainstream around Toy Story 3's release in 2010. The phrase echoes Coleridge's "Water, water, everywhere" from The Rime of the Ancient Mariner. Common misuse: people sometimes put the full sentence ("Bugs, bugs everywhere") all in one box rather than splitting it, losing the visual rhythm. The widescreen variant (showing a wider shot of the Buzz Lightyear aisle scene) is a separate but related template. Note: "Woody" was removed from tags — Woody does not appear in the standard template image.

[yall-got-any-more-of-that]
name = Y'all Got Any More Of That
description = Used when you want to express an exaggerated, almost desperate craving for something — a food, a feeling, a type of content, a life experience — humorously comparing yourself to a twitchy addict who just needs one more hit. The joke lands because the thing being craved is usually mundane or relatable, not actually drugs.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (thing being craved): The category or subject you're obsessively craving more of — often phrased as "Y'all got any more of them [X]" or just the thing itself, e.g. "sleep", "good TV shows", "Friday afternoons". Box 2 (specific craving or punchline): The more specific version, qualifier, or punchline that lands the joke, e.g. "where nothing bad happens", "that hit different", "where I'm not the problem".
box_labels = thing being craved, specific craving or punchline
tags = craving, addiction, desperation, wanting more, relatable, humorous, self-deprecating, Chappelle's Show, Tyrone Biggums, need, obsession, give me more
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/21uy0f.jpg
tone = self-deprecating
notes = The image is Tyrone Biggums, Dave Chappelle's crack-addict character, with a twitchy, intensely pleading expression — the humor comes from comparing yourself to a desperate addict over something completely mundane. No text is baked into the image; both boxes are fully customizable. A common misuse is treating Box 2 as "who's asking" — it is not about the asker, it's the punchline or qualifier for the craving. Some variants rephrase the top box to skip "y'all got any more of" entirely and just name the thing directly, letting the image carry the addictive-craving framing. The meme originated circa 2010 on MemeGenerator and broke out on Reddit in 2013-2014.

[you-guys-are-getting-paid]
name = You Guys are Getting Paid
description = Used when you discover that other people have been receiving something (payment, sleep, a benefit, a perk) that you had no idea existed — expressing oblivious, almost innocent shock at having been left out of something everyone else apparently knew about.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (what others are getting): Describe what the other people or groups are receiving — the thing the shocked person is about to learn about. Box 2 (shocked reaction): The realization line, typically phrased as "You guys are getting [X]?" or "Wait… you guys are getting paid?" — expressing genuine, comedic obliviousness.
box_labels = what others are getting, shocked reaction
tags = shock, obliviousness, unpaid labor, discovering inequality, self-deprecating, compensation, left out, naive, we're the millers, will poulter, workplace humor, envy
box_count = 2
image_url = https://i.imgflip.com/2xscjb.png
tone = self-deprecating
notes = The original movie scene is a 4-panel cascading format where each character reveals a progressively smaller payment until Will Poulter's character discovers he is getting nothing. Many Imgflip templates preserve all 4 panels. The 2-box version collapses this into: setup (what others get) + punchline (Will Poulter's shocked face). The phrase "You guys are getting paid?" is NOT baked into the image — it is user-added text. The meme has a very popular variant format "Wait, you guys are getting [X]?" applied to anything beyond money (sleep, dates, raises, etc.). Common misuse: the humor requires genuine obliviousness from the shocked party — applying it to situations where the person was already aware of the disparity kills the joke. Box labels trimmed from verbose descriptions to short 1-4 word phrases.
